does: This paper introduces SafeProtein, the first systematic red-teaming framework designed to test protein foundation models for safety vulnerabilities. The framework combines multimodal prompt engineering with heuristic beam search to generate adversarial inputs that attempt to jailbreak models into producing proteins with biological safety risks. The authors construct SafeProtein-Bench, a manually curated benchmark dataset paired with comprehensive evaluation protocols, and demonstrate successful attacks against state-of-the-art models including ESM3, achieving up to 70% attack success rates. The work empirically reveals that current protein foundation models can be manipulated to generate potentially harmful biological outputs despite their intended design for protein understanding and design tasks.
connects: This work extends the red-teaming paradigm, well-established in large language model safety research, to the emerging domain of protein foundation models. It responds to growing concerns about misuse of increasingly powerful protein design systems like ESM3, ProtBERT, and AlphaFold-based models, building on prior frameworks for adversarial testing of neural systems. SafeProtein combines techniques from prompt engineering—developed extensively for LLM jailbreaking—with molecular-specific heuristic search methods, adapting safety evaluation practices from the NLP community to the protein domain. The work acknowledges prior biosecurity concerns in synthetic biology while addressing the gap where protein models lacked systematic safety evaluation benchmarks comparable to those applied to language models.
enables: This framework and benchmark enable future development of safer protein foundation models by identifying concrete failure modes and attack surfaces. The work provides a baseline for measuring progress in robust protein model design, supporting the development of defense mechanisms such as input filtering, output screening, and training procedures that penalize harmful generations. SafeProtein-Bench can serve as a standard evaluation resource for assessing new protein models' safety properties before deployment. The red-teaming insights may inform the design of protein models with built-in safety constraints and drive broader development of biosecurity protocols for AI systems in high-stakes domains. Future work can extend these methods to other biological AI systems and explore the tension between model capability and safety in protein design applications.
